refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe remote operation by tele-operators.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety margins. The p95 percentile metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions rather than optimistic average-case assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit latency budgeting and verification.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (command transmission → vehicle sensor acquisition → telemetry reception at operator console) across representative network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) using instrumented test scenarios. Collect p95 statistics over minimum 1000 samples per condition.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire and encode vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor data) at ≥ 10 Hz minimum sampling rate.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands (movement, tool control) with deterministic latency budget allocation.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall receive and decode remote commands with bounded processing delay.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The network link shall support bidirectional communication with latency contribution ≤ 100 ms p95 under agreed conditions.
FR-NGHE-008.5: The teleoperation system shall detect and report latency violations or network degradation to safety services.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement low-latency teleoperation architecture with hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding, dedicated network priority queuing, and predictive state extrapolation to mask network jitter.
DP-B: Deploy edge computing nodes at site perimeter to reduce round-trip distance, implement local command buffering with conflict detection, and use adaptive bitrate streaming.

DP-C: Establish dedicated low-latency network slice (5G/private network) with QoS guarantees, implement deterministic scheduling on vehicle and operator console, and use protocol optimization (UDP-based custom stack vs. TCP).

DP-D: Hybrid approach combining network optimization (DP-C foundation) with local predictive control (DP-B edge logic) and hardware acceleration (DP-A encoding), with fallback to reduced-capability mode if latency exceeds threshold.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Hybrid multi-layer latency optimization combining (1) dedicated network slice with QoS guarantees and deterministic scheduling, (2) edge computing for predictive state extrapolation and local safety validation, and (3) hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding with adaptive bitrate control. Implement latency monitoring and graceful degradation to safe reduced-capability mode if p95 > 150 ms.

potential_sources: Network slice provisioning couples to site infrastructure (external dependency, not a coupling defect if clearly specified).
Edge computing placement couples to site topology and network architecture (manageable via interface specification).
Hardware acceleration couples to vehicle compute platform selection (acceptable; vehicle platform is a known design parameter).
Fallback mode couples to safety services for mode transition authorization (acceptable; SafetyAlert interface handles this).
coupling_check.notes: DP-D exhibits acceptable coupling to external infrastructure and safety services. No internal circular dependencies detected. Coupling is managed through explicit interface contracts (Network Link, SafetyAlert, Telemetry).

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Site network infrastructure can support dedicated QoS slice or equivalent priority mechanism.
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle compute platform has sufficient capacity for hardware-accelerated encoding (GPU or ASIC available).
ASSUMPTION: Edge computing nodes can be deployed at site with acceptable latency to vehicle (< 50 ms round-trip).
RISK: If site network is shared/congested, QoS guarantees may not be achievable; mitigation requires network upgrade or fallback to reduced-capability mode.
RISK: Hardware acceleration adds cost and complexity; if unavailable, software encoding may exceed latency budget.
RISK: Predictive extrapolation can diverge from actual vehicle state if network outage occurs; requires safety validation of extrapolated commands.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify network infrastructure assumptions in system requirements. Define fallback mode capabilities and safety constraints explicitly. Specify hardware acceleration requirements (GPU/ASIC type, performance targets). Add latency budget allocation table across encoding, network, decoding, and safety validation stages.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns end-to-end teleoperation latency performance. Encompasses command reception, encoding/decoding, state management, network interface adaptation, and latency monitoring. Responsible for maintaining p95 ≤ 150 ms and reporting violations to Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, latency metrics) to operator console. Frequency ≥ 10 Hz. Includes timestamp for latency calculation.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator commands (movement vectors, tool control, mode requests) from remote operator console. Latency-critical path; must be processed within allocated budget.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module reports latency violations (p95 > 150 ms), network degradation, or mode transitions to Safety Services. Triggers fallback to reduced-capability mode if necessary.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional network interface for teleoperation traffic. Teleoperation module manages QoS tagging, adaptive bitrate, and latency monitoring. Receives network status (jitter, packet loss, available bandwidth) from network layer.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container. Teleoperation is a major subsystem but not the entire solution. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse teleoperation ownership across multiple concerns (vehicle control, safety, maintenance). Not recommended as primary owner.

Vehicle module owns vehicle-side hardware and control logic. Teleoperation latency is a system-level concern spanning vehicle, network, and operator console. Vehicle module would own only the vehicle-side encoding and command processing, not the full end-to-end latency. Partial containment; diffusion risk high.

Teleoperation module is purpose-built for this requirement. Owns encoding/decoding, command processing, state management, network adaptation, and latency monitoring. Clear ownership boundary. Minimal diffusion; all latency-critical logic is contained. Excellent fit.

Safety Services owns safety validation and mode authorization. Teleoperation latency monitoring is a safety concern, but the latency optimization logic (encoding, network adaptation, predictive extrapolation) is not a safety service responsibility. Assigning here would diffuse performance engineering. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of CR-NGHE-008. It has clear responsibility for end-to-end latency performance, owns the critical path (encoding → network → decoding), and interfaces with Vehicle (for state acquisition), Network Link (for transmission), and Safety Services (for violation reporting). Containment score 0.95 reflects strong alignment.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module depends on Vehicle module for timely state acquisition. If Vehicle state sampling is delayed, Teleoperation cannot meet latency budget. Mitigation: Define Vehicle state interface with guaranteed latency SLA.

RISK: Teleoperation module depends on Network Link for QoS guarantees. If network infrastructure does not support dedicated slice, latency target may be unachievable. Mitigation: Clarify network assumptions; define fallback mode.

RISK: Teleoperation module must coordinate with Safety Services for mode transitions. If Safety Services validation adds latency, end-to-end budget is impacted. Mitigation: Allocate latency budget to safety validation; use asynchronous reporting where possible.
RISK: Hardware acceleration (GPU/ASIC) may not be available on all vehicle platforms. Mitigation: Define minimum hardware requirements; specify software fallback with degraded performance.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish explicit latency budget allocation: Vehicle state acquisition (≤ 20 ms), Teleoperation encoding (≤ 30 ms), Network transmission (≤ 50 ms), Teleoperation decoding (≤ 30 ms), Safety validation (≤ 20 ms). Define Vehicle state interface SLA. Specify Network Link QoS requirements. Document hardware acceleration requirements and software fallback strategy.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid multi-layer optimization) directly addresses FR-NGHE-008 (p95 ≤ 150 ms teleoperation latency). DP-D provides three complementary mechanisms: (1) network optimization for infrastructure-level latency reduction, (2) edge computing for predictive masking of network jitter, (3) hardware acceleration for deterministic encoding/decoding. DP-D includes explicit fallback to safe reduced-capability mode if latency target is not met, addressing safety criticality. No independence violations detected; DP-D is a coherent solution concept.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module owns encoding/decoding and network adaptation, but depends on Vehicle module for state acquisition and Safety Services for mode authorization. Dependencies are manageable via interface contracts (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link).

Edge computing placement (DP-D component) may require coordination with site infrastructure team. Not a diffusion risk if edge nodes are treated as external infrastructure (not owned by Teleoperation module).
Hardware acceleration requirement (DP-D component) couples to Vehicle platform selection. Acceptable coupling; Vehicle module is a known design parameter.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score. All latency-critical logic (encoding, decoding, network adaptation, monitoring) is contained within Teleoperation. External dependencies (Vehicle state, Network Link, Safety Services) are managed through explicit interfaces. No diffusion of teleoperation concerns across other modules.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via instrumented testing. Measure end-to-end latency (command transmission → vehicle state acquisition → telemetry reception) across representative network conditions. Collect p95 statistics over ≥ 1000 samples per condition. Verify fallback mode activation when latency exceeds threshold. Verification requires test infrastructure (network emulation, instrumented vehicle, operator console) but is straightforward.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear. Teleoperation module is assigned ownership of CR-NGHE-008. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through FR-NGHE-008.5) are allocated to Teleoperation (encoding, decoding, command processing, monitoring) and Network Link (network latency contribution). Safety Services is allocated FR-NGHE-008.5 (latency violation reporting). Clear ownership boundaries; no ambiguity.

recommended_revisions: Establish explicit latency budget allocation across encoding, network, decoding, and safety validation stages. Document in design specification.
Define Vehicle state interface SLA (sampling rate, latency guarantee). Ensure Vehicle module commits to ≤ 20 ms state acquisition latency.
Clarify network infrastructure assumptions. Specify QoS requirements (dedicated slice, priority queuing, bandwidth guarantee). Define fallback strategy if QoS is unavailable.
Document hardware acceleration requirements (GPU/ASIC type, performance targets). Specify software fallback strategy if hardware is unavailable.
Define reduced-capability mode: what operations are allowed, what safety constraints apply, how operator is notified. Coordinate with Safety Services.
Add latency monitoring and reporting requirements to Teleoperation module. Specify SafetyAlert interface for violation reporting.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-008 is not atomic. It requires decomposition into sub-FRs addressing distinct concerns: (1) vehicle state acquisition latency, (2) command transmission latency, (3) encoding/decoding latency, (4) network latency, (5) latency monitoring and fallback. DP-D (hybrid optimization) is sound and well-justified, but implementation requires detailed design of each sub-component. Teleoperation module assignment is correct and well-contained. Proceeding to next decomposition level is necessary to allocate latency budgets, specify interfaces, and detail fallback logic.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire and encode vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor data) at ≥ 10 Hz minimum sampling rate with encoding latency ≤ 30 ms p95.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands (movement, tool control) with deterministic latency budget allocation ≤ 30 ms p95 for command processing.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall receive and decode remote commands with decoding latency ≤ 30 ms p95.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The network link shall support bidirectional communication with latency contribution ≤ 50 ms p95 under agreed network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss).
FR-NGHE-008.5: The teleoperation system shall detect latency violations (p95 > 150 ms) and report to Safety Services for mode transition authorization within ≤ 20 ms.

assumptions: Site network infrastructure can support dedicated QoS slice or equivalent priority mechanism.
Vehicle compute platform has sufficient capacity for hardware-accelerated encoding (GPU or ASIC available).
Edge computing nodes can be deployed at site with acceptable latency to vehicle (< 50 ms round-trip).
Vehicle state acquisition latency is ≤ 20 ms (to be confirmed with Vehicle module design).
Safety Services can authorize mode transitions within ≤ 20 ms (to be confirmed with Safety Services design).
Operator console has sufficient compute capacity for hardware-accelerated decoding.
Network conditions are stable and predictable (jitter, packet loss within agreed bounds).
open_risks: RISK: If site network is shared/congested, QoS guarantees may not be achievable. Mitigation: Network upgrade or fallback to reduced-capability mode.
RISK: Hardware acceleration may not be available on all vehicle platforms. Mitigation: Define minimum hardware requirements; specify software fallback with degraded performance.
RISK: Predictive extrapolation can diverge from actual vehicle state if network outage occurs. Mitigation: Safety validation of extrapolated commands; timeout mechanism to revert to safe state.
RISK: Edge computing placement may introduce additional latency if not co-located with network gateway. Mitigation: Detailed site survey and network topology analysis.
RISK: Fallback mode may not be acceptable to operator (reduced capability). Mitigation: Define fallback mode capabilities; coordinate with Safety Manager and Site Manager.
evidence_needed: Network infrastructure assessment: QoS capabilities, available bandwidth, typical jitter/packet loss under agreed conditions.
Vehicle platform specification: compute capacity, available GPU/ASIC, state acquisition latency.
Operator console specification: compute capacity, display latency, input device latency.
Site topology and network diagram: edge computing placement options, distance to vehicle, network path analysis.
Safety requirements for reduced-capability mode: what operations are allowed, what constraints apply.
Latency measurement methodology: instrumentation approach, test scenarios, statistical analysis (p95 calculation).
Hardware acceleration options: GPU/ASIC availability, performance benchmarks, cost/power trade-offs.
Fallback mode design: trigger conditions, mode transition logic, operator notification, safety validation.
